title: Error-corrected phase estimation averaged over variable grids on a trapped-ion quantum computer: hyperacuity spectra of a CO molecule adsorbed onto $χ$-Fe$_5$C$_2$
authors: Taichi Kosugi, Hirofumi Nishi, Keito Kasebayashi, Hiroki Takahashi, Yu-ichiro Matsushita
abstract: Quantum phase estimation (QPE) is an underlying technology for extracting the excitation spectra of many-electron systems, yet its practical use on current hardware is hindered by low grid resolution and environmental noises. Here we propose QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG), a vernier-type approach that combines low-resolution QPE with multiple origin shifts and physically motivated continuous parametrization to reconstruct the spectra accurately. We introduce this approach into an end-to-end workflow for the {\it ab initio}-based model system for a CO molecule adsorbed onto the $χ$-Fe$_5$C$_2$ surface. We perform experiments on Quantinuum H2-2 using both physical QPE circuits and logical QPE circuits encoded in the Steane code with offline bit-flip correction. We demonstrate that QAVG accurately reconstructs the spectra with deviations much smaller than the nominal QPE resolution, even when the noisy histograms are used. The cost landscapes averaged over the shifted grids substantially suppress the local minima arising from the spectral leakage, thereby stabilizing the optimization of trial parameters. These results indicate that QAVG provides a robust route to quantum simulations of correlated spectra toward the era of early-fault-tolerant quantum computers.
summary: This paper introduces QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG) as a robust technique to overcome noise and low resolution in quantum simulations of electronic spectra. By applying QAVG to a model of CO adsorption on a metal surface, the authors demonstrate that the method accurately reconstructs the system's Density of States using both physical and error-corrected logical quantum circuits. This paves a path for simulating complex correlated materials on early fault-tolerant quantum computers.
main_problem: The core challenge is accurately extracting the excitation spectra of many-electron systems using Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE), which is limited by low grid resolution and environmental noise on current quantum hardware.
main_result: The proposed QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG) method successfully reconstructs spectra with high accuracy, even when using noisy measurements from physical and logical quantum circuits.
method: The authors developed QAVG, a vernier-type approach combining low-resolution QPE with multiple origin shifts and continuous parametrization to stabilize spectral reconstruction.
model_or_system: The study applies this method to model the electronic structure of a CO molecule adsorbed onto a $\chi$-Fe$_5$C$_2$ surface, simplified to a two-orbital dimer model for feasibility.
key_observables: Hyperacuity spectra, one-particle Green's functions (GFs), and the Density of States (DOS).
important_parameters: The chemical potential shift ($\Delta\mu$) was set to ensure a two-electron ground state; the calculation uses the Steane code for logical qubits.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that averaging over multiple energy origins (QAVG) can suppress spectral leakage and stabilize the optimization process, making the method robust for early fault-tolerant quantum computing.
figures_summary: Figures show the unit cell and DOS from DFT, comparisons of DOS from QAVG vs. FCI/noiseless simulations, and cost landscapes demonstrating the suppression of local minima via averaging.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical model (CO/$\chi$-Fe$_5$C$_2$) and the need for spectral extraction. It then details the QAVG methodology, implementing it on both physical and logical quantum circuits (Steane code) to calculate the GF and DOS, culminating in a comparison of results against theoretical benchmarks.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the practical limitations of quantum simulation by proposing a noise-resilient algorithm (QAVG) that makes high-resolution spectroscopy feasible on near-term, noisy quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Unit cell used for DFT calculations in the present study. This periodic system mimics the non-periodic adsorption system, where a CO molecule is adsorbed onto the (510) surface of χ-Fe5C2. This figure was drawn by using VESTA [51]. (b) Schematic energy diagram of the molecular orbitals (MOs) of an isolated CO molecule. The amplitudes of the MOs are also depicted. (c) Electronic density of states (DOS) obtained in the DFT calculation as functions of an energy measured from the Fermi level. The total DOS is shown as black curves. The projected DOS onto the atomic orbitals (AOs) at the CO molecule are also shown, where the MOs giving rise to the major contributions are written. The...

FIG. 2. (a) Convention for the Steane code adopted in the present study. The seven vertices represent the constituent physical qubits forming a logical qubit. The four vertices forming each plaquette of a single color designate the stabilizer generator for each of X- and Z-types [see Eq. (25)]. This figure does not necessarily represent the actual layout of qubits on hardware. (b) The FT encoding circuit Uenc [69] for preparing |0⟩L from initialized physical qubits. Its former part, that encodes |0⟩L, is non-FT. The latter part prevents a harmful error from propagating outward the eight-qubit circuit by detecting it via the ancillary state |1⟩as a flag. This performs QPE for n(QFT) q = 3 by using three ancillary qubits with Rk ≡diag(1, exp(−i2π/2k)). FIG. 4. (a) Five MLWOs constructed from the DFT calculation. (b) Electronic DOS originating from the interpolated Wannier bands are shown as shaded region. The total DOS and projected DOS in this figure are the same as in Fig. 1. The energy range between the two vertical dashed lines is the energy window for the construction of the MLWOs. The vertical green solid lines indicate the orbital energies of the MLWOs.
